macro_segments.title: The Four Modes of Execution: Hereg (Decapitation by Sword)

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's description of hereg and the debate between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages
Historical context: Roman execution methods and the progression from axe to sword
Rabbi Yehuda's objection to mimicking secular government practices and the prohibition of lo titnu bechukoteihem
The Sages' response: When a practice is Torah-derived, it is not considered mimicking gentiles
Extended discussion of lo titnu bechukoteihem: intent, derivation, and idolatry-connected practices

macro_segments.title: Identifying the Source of Hereg: The Murderer and the Kal Vachomer from Killing a Slave

micro_segments.title: The categories of people executed by hereg: the murderer and the people of the idolatrous city
The murderer's execution derived from the phrase 'Nekem Yinakeim' (vengeance shall be taken)
Understanding 'Nekima' as sword through the curse: 'Cherev Nokemes Nekam Brit'

macro_segments.title: The Paradigm of Egla Arufa and Its Application to Murder

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's principle: Ve-Ahavta Le-Reiacha Kamocha—ensuring the most respectful death possible

macro_segments.title: Identifying the Source of Hereg: The Murderer and the Kal Vachomer from Killing a Slave (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Kal Vachomer: if killing a slave warrants the sword, killing a free person warrants it more
Debate over whether Kal Vachomer applies given uncertainty about the relative severity of executions

macro_segments.title: The Paradigm of Egla Arufa and Its Application to Murder (continued)

micro_segments.title: Linking all murderers to the Egla Arufa paradigm: 'Hukshu Kol Shofchei Damim Le-Egla Arufa'
The method from Egla Arufa: using a sword at the neck, compared to using an axe

macro_segments.title: Chenek (Strangulation): The Generic Form of Execution

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's description of chenek: immersion in dung, wrapping with soft and hard materials, pulling
Textual analysis of 'Mot Yumat' (death): does it refer to a specific form or a generic execution?
Rabbi Yehoshua's alternative view: 'Mot Yumat' means the lightest form of execution by default

macro_segments.title: The Tradition of Four Executions and the Assignment of Generic Cases to Chenek

micro_segments.title: The logical corollary: if execution is unspecified in Torah, we cannot impose a heavier form

macro_segments.title: Chenek (Strangulation): The Generic Form of Execution (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yonatan's view: 'Mot Yumat' refers to executions that resemble natural death, without visible marks
Rav Yonatan's derivation from 'Misah Bidei Shamayim' (death by Heaven's hand): no disfigurement

macro_segments.title: The Tradition of Four Executions and the Assignment of Generic Cases to Chenek (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava's assertion of a tradition: there are exactly four modes of execution
Rashi's explanation: the four modes are known by tradition, and unspecified cases are assigned to the lightest

macro_segments.title: Determining the Basis for Sekilah: Demihem Bam vs. Mot Yumasu

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Zeira's question to Abbaye: which phrase designates sekilah—'Mot Yumasu' or 'Demihem Bam'?
The paradigm case: Ov and Yidoni explicitly receive sekilah and include both phrases
Abbaye's answer: 'Demihem Bam' (their blood is upon them) is the key phrase for sekilah
Resolution: 'Mot Yumasu' provides license to execute by any available means when standard execution fails
Why 'Mot Yumasu' cannot be the sekilah indicator: it appears in non-sekilah cases like adultery

macro_segments.title: The Categories of Those Receiving Sekilah and Introduction to Individual Cases

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's list of sekilah cases: sexual transgressions (mother, stepmother, daughter-in-law, animals), idolatry, blasphemy, witchcraft, and parental dishonor
Rashi's explanation for why parental dishonor (cursing parents) is sekilah rather than lighter: it involves disgracing the parents and defaming God's name
The case of lying with one's mother: double liability if she is also the father's wife
Rabbi Yehuda's position in the Mishna: a mother is never classified as 'father's wife'; father's wife means stepmother only

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Limiting View of Father's Wife: The Braita's Narrower Debate

micro_segments.title: The Braita's version of Rabbi Yehuda: 'If she was not fit for the father, liability only for mother'
Interpretation: 'not fit' refers to marriages that are valid only rabbinically (negative prohibitions) but not biblically
Rabbi Yehuda apparently holds like Rabbi Akiva: Kiddushin is not binding even for negative prohibitions
Rav Oshaia's challenge: if Rabbi Yehuda held that, women with negative prohibitions would have no Chalitza, contradicting established practice
Resolution: Rabbi Yehuda may agree with the Halacha but disputes the terminology (Issur Mitzvah vs. Issur Kedusha)

macro_segments.title: Returning to the Mishna's Simpler Position: Mother Cannot Be 'Father's Wife'

micro_segments.title: Rav Yitzchak brings a Braita from Eretz Yisrael matching the Mishna's plainer interpretation
Abbaye's explanation: the verse 'Imcha Hi' (she is your mother) limits application to the mother category only

topical_tags.term: lo titnu bechukoteihem (do not go in their ways)
mot yumat (shall surely be put to death)
demihem bam (their blood is upon them)
ve-ahavta le-reiacha kamocha (love your fellow as yourself)
misah bidei shamayim (death by Heaven's hand)
